_comment: WIN-ACT-001/§3 icon buttons -- WIN-ACT-003. Composition-PINNING contract for the standalone
icon-only close/dismiss/reset affordance across the sfx2 feedback + property-chip surfaces.
docs/design/02-actions.md 3.1 states a native standalone icon-button part does not exist in
definition.xml; native drawing reuses the toolbar/Button face where the control is a toolbar
item, and falls through to the generic pushbutton tonal-pill part otherwise. This contract
proves in source exactly which shared part each real consumer rides today -- it does NOT add
the missing native icon-button part.
Two host classes, verified by direct source reading:
Class A (toolbar-hosted): a GtkToolButton inside a GtkToolbar carrying the small-button
style class, built via weld_toolbar(...) -- rides the already-compiled toolbar/Button part.
-> infobar 'close' (sfx2/source/dialog/infobar.cxx) and propertychip 'remove'
(sfx2/source/dialog/mgetempl.cxx).
Class B (plain-button-hosted): a GtkButton with a GtkImage child and no label, built via
weld_button(...) -- falls through to the pushbutton/Entire tonal-pill part because no
dedicated icon-button extra state exists (definition.xml declares only extra=action/flat).
-> notificationcard 'dismiss_button' (sfx2/source/notification/NotificationCard.cxx) and
notificationmanager 'close_button' (sfx2/source/notification/NotificationManagerController.cxx).
Accessible name (design 3.3: an unnamed icon button fails review): all four rely on a non-empty
translatable tooltip-text as the accessible-name channel; only notificationcard's dismiss_button
additionally calls set_accessible_name explicitly. The contract pins the tooltip-text channel on
all four and the explicit call where it exists.
Closed-world scan: an independent git ls-files *.ui walk finds every icon-only GtkButton/
GtkToolButton whose glyph is 'window-close-symbolic' (the shared close/dismiss/reset glyph); the
discovered set must equal the four pinned consumers plus the one excluded candidate (deck.ui
btn_close, a sidebar-deck close owned by the sidebar surface). A new unclassified window-close
icon button fails closed; a pinned/excluded entry the scanner no longer sees fails closed.
Source evidence only: runtime_verified is false throughout -- no native build, icon-button pixels,
or runtime interaction are claimed.
design_reference: docs/design/02-actions.md (3. Icon buttons: 3.1 anatomy, 3.2 states, 3.3 accessibility)

consumers.note: Rides the compiled toolbar/Button part (WIN-ACT-002). Icon also set at runtime via set_item_image; the .ui icon-name is the pinned declared glyph.
Falls through to the pushbutton/Entire tonal-pill part (no icon-only extra state exists). Gets an explicit set_accessible_name in addition to the tooltip.
Falls through to the pushbutton/Entire tonal-pill part (no icon-only extra state exists); tooltip-text is the sole accessible-name channel.

excluded_candidates.reason: The sidebar deck close button belongs to the sidebar deck surface (05-navigation / sidebar rows), not this feedback/property-chip icon-button family; discovered by the shared window-close glyph but out of this row's scope.

pushbutton.note: Class-B consumers fall through to this part. If any extra value other than action/flat appears (a dedicated icon-only state), the Class-B fallback classification is stale and this fails closed.

toolbar_button.note: Class-A consumers ride this part (WIN-ACT-002); it must stay resolvable with an enabled state.
